lines.en: Apparently, the figures from the last quarter were not included. Perhaps they were not available yet.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Estavam, sim. Portanto foi uma escolha, não um esquecimento.
lines.en: They were available. So it was a choice, not an oversight.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nesse caso, tudo indica que quiseram apresentar um quadro mais favorável.
lines.en: In that case, everything suggests they wanted to present a more favourable picture.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: É o que eu suspeitava. Achas que devo levantar a questão na reunião?
lines.en: That is what I suspected. Do you think I should raise the issue in the meeting?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Talvez valha a pena perguntar em privado primeiro. Diria que é mais prudente.
lines.en: Perhaps it is worth asking in private first. I would say that is more prudent.

days.title: Os conectores complexos: contudo, todavia, ora, aliás
days.objective: You can link written arguments with contudo, todavia, ora and aliás, choosing each one according to whether you are contrasting, reasoning or reinforcing.
days.paretoFocus: These four connectors are the backbone of opinion writing in Portugal and instantly lift a text above the mas and também level.
drills: Write one paragraph of four sentences in which each sentence begins with a different one of these four connectors.
Take a text you already wrote and replace every mas with either contudo or todavia, then check the tone.
Write two sentences where the second one reinforces the first with aliás.

items: What is the difference in register between mas and contudo?
Which connector introduces a step in the speaker's reasoning?
Does aliás introduce a contrast or an extra point on the same side?

activities.title: Ligar ideias com precisão
activities.intro: At this level the choice of connector carries meaning. Contudo and todavia mark contrast in a formal register, ora moves the reasoning forward one step, and aliás adds a further point that strengthens what you just said. Note the commas: these connectors are normally set off by punctuation.

items.hr: O projeto é interessante; contudo, é demasiado caro.
items.en: The project is interesting; however, it is too expensive.
items.note: contudo is the neutral formal equivalent of mas

items.hr: Todavia, ninguém apresentou uma alternativa credível.
items.en: Nevertheless, nobody put forward a credible alternative.
items.note: todavia is slightly more literary than contudo, same meaning

items.hr: Ora, se ninguém se opôs, o assunto está encerrado.
items.en: Now then, if nobody objected, the matter is closed.
items.note: ora introduces the next step of an argument, often before a conclusion

items.hr: Ele nunca esteve lá; aliás, nem conhece a cidade.
items.en: He has never been there; in fact, he does not even know the city.
items.note: aliás adds a stronger point in the same direction

items.hr: A proposta foi aprovada. Aliás, foi aprovada por unanimidade.
items.en: The proposal was approved. In fact, it was approved unanimously.
items.note: aliás can also correct upwards, adding precision

activities.title: Escolher o conector certo
activities.intro: Practise matching each connector to the logical relation it expresses.

questions.prompt: Which connector adds a second, stronger point on the same side of the argument?
questions.explanation: Aliás reinforces what was just said, while contudo and todavia introduce a contrast and embora introduces a concessive clause.

questions.prompt: What does 'Ora' signal at the start of a sentence in an argument?
options: the next step in the speaker's reasoning
a total reversal of the previous idea
an example of what was just claimed
questions.answer: the next step in the speaker's reasoning
questions.explanation: Ora moves the argument forward, typically setting up a premise that leads to a conclusion in the same or the following sentence.

questions.prompt: Complete with the formal connector meaning 'however' that begins with the letter c: 'A ideia é boa; ___, faltam meios para a pôr em prática.'
questions.explanation: Contudo marks a contrast between the value of the idea and the lack of resources, in a register suited to written argument.

questions.prompt: Complete with the more literary synonym of the connector in the previous question, beginning with the letter t: 'Prometeram mudanças; ___, tudo ficou na mesma.'
questions.explanation: Todavia expresses the same contrast but belongs to a slightly higher, more literary register.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'The proposal is good; however, it arrives too late.'
questions.explanation: The semicolon joins the two clauses and contudo takes a comma after it when it opens the second clause.

activities.title: Discussão sobre a nova ciclovia
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a neighbour argue about a new cycle lane, using formal connectors to structure your points.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vais dizer-me que a ciclovia nova foi boa ideia?
lines.en: Are you going to tell me the new cycle lane was a good idea?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Acho que sim. Tirou carros do centro; contudo, admito que a obra demorou tempo de mais.
lines.en: I think so. It took cars out of the centre; however, I admit the works took far too long.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Demorou um ano inteiro. Todavia, ninguém pediu desculpa aos comerciantes.
lines.en: It took a whole year. Nevertheless, nobody apologised to the shopkeepers.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ora, se as vendas caíram durante a obra, faz sentido que estejam revoltados.
lines.en: Now then, if sales fell during the works, it makes sense that they are angry.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Caíram, sim. Aliás, dois cafés fecharam nessa rua.
lines.en: They did fall. In fact, two cafés on that street closed.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Isso é grave. Contudo, os que ficaram dizem agora que têm mais clientes ao fim de semana.
lines.en: That is serious. However, the ones that stayed now say they have more customers at the weekend.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Pode ser. Ora, se assim é, o problema foi a obra e não a ciclovia.
lines.en: That may be. Now then, if that is so, the problem was the works and not the cycle lane.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: É exatamente o que eu defendo. Aliás, era isso que devia ter sido explicado desde o início.
lines.en: That is exactly what I am arguing. In fact, that is what should have been explained from the start.

days.title: Os registos: erudito, corrente, familiar
days.objective: You can say the same thing in three registers, choosing institutional vocabulary for formal writing, neutral vocabulary for everyday exchanges and slang only among friends.
days.paretoFocus: Register mistakes are the fastest way to sound foreign, because a learner who writes an email like a text message reads as careless rather than as non-native.
drills: Take one simple request and write it three times: for a public office, for a colleague and for a close friend.
List five verbs you use daily and find their institutional equivalent.
Rewrite a short informal message so that every slang word disappears.

items: Which verb replaces pedir in institutional letters?
What is the neutral everyday way of saying you are leaving?
Name one slang expression that must never appear in a written complaint.

activities.title: A mesma ideia em três registos
activities.intro: Portuguese separates three broad registers. The erudite register uses long Latinate verbs and impersonal forms and belongs to letters, contracts and speeches. The current register is what you use with colleagues and strangers. The familiar register carries slang and clipped forms and is limited to friends and family.

items.hr: Retiro-me, com a vossa licença.
items.en: I shall withdraw, with your permission.
items.note: erudito: ceremonial, almost never heard between friends

items.hr: Vou-me embora, tenho de apanhar o autocarro.
items.en: I am off, I have to catch the bus.
items.note: corrente: the neutral everyday choice

items.hr: Olha, eu vou bazar que já é tarde.
items.en: Look, I am going to clear off, it is late.
items.note: familiar: bazar is spoken slang in Portugal, never written formally

items.hr: Solicitamos o envio da documentação em falta.
items.en: We request that the missing documentation be sent.
items.note: erudito: solicitar replaces pedir in institutional writing

items.hr: Podes mandar-me os papéis?
items.en: Can you send me the paperwork?
items.note: corrente: the same request between colleagues

activities.title: Ajustar o registo à situação
activities.intro: Practise choosing the register that fits the reader.

questions.prompt: Which sentence belongs to the erudite register?
options: Solicitamos a vossa melhor atenção para o assunto.
Vê lá se olhas para isto, está bem?
Podes dar uma vista de olhos nisto?
questions.answer: Solicitamos a vossa melhor atenção para o assunto.
questions.explanation: Solicitar plus the possessive vossa is typical of institutional correspondence, while the other three belong to spoken registers.

questions.prompt: You are writing to your landlord about a broken boiler. Which opening is appropriate?
options: Venho por este meio informar que o esquentador se avariou.
O esquentador está feito num oito.
Olhe lá, isto não aquece nada.
questions.answer: Venho por este meio informar que o esquentador se avariou.
questions.explanation: Venho por este meio informar is the standard formula for a written notification; the other options are spoken and familiar.

questions.prompt: Rewrite in the institutional register, replacing the verb 'pedir' in 'Pedimos o envio dos documentos': '___ o envio dos documentos.'
questions.explanation: Solicitar is the formal counterpart of pedir and is expected in letters from and to public bodies.

questions.prompt: Complete with the everyday European Portuguese word for a city bus: 'Perdi o ___ das oito e cheguei tarde.'
questions.explanation: Autocarro is the word used in Portugal for a city bus; other varieties of Portuguese use different terms.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'We would be grateful if you could confirm your attendance.'
questions.explanation: Agradecemos que is followed by the conjuntivo, giving confirme, and is a standard formal request formula.

activities.title: O email e a mensagem
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend is helping you decide how formal an email to an institution should be, and you keep switching between registers.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mostra lá o email que escreveste para a câmara.
lines.en: Let me see the email you wrote to the town hall.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Começa assim: venho por este meio solicitar informação sobre o processo.
lines.en: It starts like this: I hereby request information about the case.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Está bem. Mas depois escreveste que a coisa está a demorar imenso. Isso é familiar de mais.
lines.en: That is fine. But then you wrote that the thing is taking ages. That is far too familiar.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tens razão. Ponho antes: o processo encontra-se pendente há vários meses.
lines.en: You are right. I will put instead: the case has been pending for several months.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Muito melhor. E no fim não podes despedir-te com um abraço.
lines.en: Much better. And at the end you cannot sign off with a hug.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Então escrevo com os melhores cumprimentos. Ao meu irmão é que mandava só um abraço.
lines.en: Then I will write kind regards. To my brother I would just send a hug.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Exatamente. Ao teu irmão até podias dizer que vais bazar cedo.
lines.en: Exactly. To your brother you could even say you are clearing off early.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A ele digo isso; à câmara escrevo que me ausentarei por motivos profissionais.
lines.en: To him I say that; to the town hall I write that I shall be absent for professional reasons.

days.title: O implícito e o subentendido
days.objective: You can read and produce meanings that are suggested rather than stated, using particles such as até and expressions such as não propriamente and se calhar.
days.paretoFocus: Much of what people in Portugal actually mean is carried by understatement, so missing the implicature means missing a refusal, a criticism or an instruction.
drills: Write three polite refusals that never contain the word não.
Take a negative opinion and express it with an understatement instead of a direct adjective.
Listen to any Portuguese conversation and note one sentence where the speaker clearly meant more than the words said.

items: What extra meaning does até add before an adjective?
How would you refuse an invitation without saying no?
What does não propriamente signal about the speaker's judgement?

activities.title: Dizer sem dizer
activities.intro: Speakers routinely mean more than they say, and Portuguese has a small set of markers that do this work. Particles like até flag a concession the listener did not expect, and negative understatements soften criticism into something almost polite. Reading these correctly is a listening skill as much as a grammar one.

items.hr: Ele até é simpático.
items.en: He is actually quite nice.
items.note: até signals that this contradicts what the listener might assume

items.hr: Não sei se será boa ideia.
items.en: I do not think that is a good idea.
items.note: a hedged doubt used as a polite refusal

items.hr: Cheguei a pensar nisso, mas depois desisti.
items.en: I did consider that at one point, but then I gave up on it.
items.note: chegar a plus infinitive means it even got as far as

items.hr: Ela não é propriamente uma especialista.
items.en: She is not exactly an expert.
items.note: não propriamente turns a blunt negative into an understatement

items.hr: Se calhar era melhor perguntares primeiro.
items.en: Maybe you should ask first.
items.note: se calhar is the European way of saying maybe, often a veiled instruction

activities.title: Ler nas entrelinhas
activities.intro: Practise decoding and producing indirect meanings.

questions.prompt: A guest says 'O jantar estava... interessante.' What is the most likely meaning?
options: The guest did not enjoy the food but is being polite.
The guest found the food genuinely delicious.
The guest wants a second helping.
The guest did not eat anything at all.
questions.answer: The guest did not enjoy the food but is being polite.
questions.explanation: The pause plus a vague positive adjective is a standard way of avoiding a direct criticism, so the listener infers the opposite.

questions.prompt: Your colleague answers your plan with 'Não sei se será boa ideia.' How should you read it?
options: as a request for more detail
as an offer to help you
questions.explanation: The apparent uncertainty is a softened negative judgement, which is how disagreement is usually expressed in a work setting.

questions.prompt: Complete with the two-word European Portuguese expression meaning 'maybe', which begins with the letter s: '___ ___ ele já saiu do escritório.'
questions.explanation: Se calhar is the everyday European equivalent of talvez and does not require the conjuntivo.

questions.prompt: Complete with the particle that signals an unexpected concession: 'O filme ___ é bom, mas é demasiado longo.'
questions.explanation: Placed before the verb, this particle tells the listener that the positive judgement goes against what they were expecting.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'She is not exactly a specialist in the subject.'
questions.explanation: Propriamente sits between the verb and the noun phrase and converts the negation into a mild criticism.

activities.title: A opinião sobre o novo colega
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague asks what you think of a new team member and you answer entirely through understatement.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Já trabalhaste com o novo colega. Que tal é ele?
lines.en: You have already worked with the new colleague. What is he like?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Até é simpático. Agora, rápido é que ele não é.
lines.en: He is actually quite nice. Fast, though, he is not.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Estás a dizer-me que ele atrasa o trabalho todo?
lines.en: Are you telling me he holds up all the work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não digo tanto. Digo que não é propriamente um exemplo de organização.
lines.en: I would not go that far. I am saying he is not exactly a model of organisation.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Percebi. E devo falar com ele ou deixo andar?
lines.en: I understand. And should I talk to him or let it go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Se calhar era melhor falares antes do próximo prazo.
lines.en: Maybe it would be better to talk before the next deadline.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Já cheguei a pensar nisso, mas achei que era cedo de mais.
lines.en: I did consider that at one point, but I thought it was too soon.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não sei se esperar será boa ideia. Enfim, tu é que sabes.
lines.en: I am not sure waiting is a good idea. Anyway, it is your call.

days.title: A ironia e o humor num texto
days.objective: You can recognise irony in Portuguese through fixed exclamations, sarcastic diminutives and the word logo, and produce a few ironic remarks yourself.
days.paretoFocus: Portuguese everyday humour is mostly ironic, so taking these sentences literally leads to real misunderstandings in conversation and in the press.
drills: Write three ironic comments about bad weather, a delayed bus and a broken appliance.
Find one fixed exclamation of complaint and use it in two different situations.
Say a sincere compliment and then the same words with an ironic intonation.

items: What does logo add to a sentence about bad timing?
How does a diminutive turn pity into sarcasm?
Which fixed exclamation means that a new problem is the last straw?

activities.title: Que sorte a minha
activities.intro: Irony in Portuguese is usually flagged by a small set of markers rather than by intonation alone. Watch for logo meaning of all times, for diminutives applied to people who deserve no pity, and for fixed exclamations of mock outrage. These appear in speech, in opinion columns and in comedy alike.

items.hr: Que sorte a minha, chover logo hoje.
items.en: Just my luck, it has to rain today of all days.
items.note: logo here means of all possible days, a classic irony marker

items.hr: Está tudo ótimo, como se vê.
items.en: Everything is going wonderfully, as you can see.
items.note: como se vê invites the listener to observe the opposite

items.hr: Ele é um génio, deixou as chaves dentro do carro.
items.en: He is a genius, he left the keys inside the car.
items.note: praise followed by evidence to the contrary

items.hr: Só faltava mais esta!
items.en: That is all I needed!
items.note: fixed exclamation of mock outrage at one more problem

items.hr: Ai, coitadinho, tem de trabalhar duas horas por dia.
items.en: Oh, poor little thing, he has to work two hours a day.
items.note: the diminutive coitadinho plus a trivial hardship signals sarcasm

activities.title: Detetar a ironia
activities.intro: Practise spotting ironic markers and building ironic remarks.

questions.prompt: What does someone mean by 'Só faltava mais esta!'?
options: One more problem has arrived and it is the last straw.
Something important is still missing from a list.
There is only one item left to buy.
The speaker is pleased with the outcome.
questions.answer: One more problem has arrived and it is the last straw.
questions.explanation: It is a frozen exclamation of exasperation and its literal meaning about something missing is no longer active.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is ironic rather than sincere?
options: Grande organização, a mala chegou três dias depois de mim.
A organização foi boa e a mala chegou comigo.
A mala chegou atrasada e reclamei no balcão.
Perdi a mala e ainda não a recuperei.
questions.answer: Grande organização, a mala chegou três dias depois de mim.
questions.explanation: The praise is immediately contradicted by the fact that follows, which is the standard structure of an ironic remark.

questions.prompt: Complete the fixed ironic exclamation meaning 'that is all I needed', using the imperfect of 'faltar': 'Só ___ mais esta!'
questions.explanation: The imperfect is frozen in this expression and cannot be replaced by the present or the preterite.

questions.prompt: Complete with the sarcastic diminutive of 'coitado': 'Ai, ___, tem de acordar às nove da manhã.'
questions.explanation: Adding -inho to a word of pity, when the hardship is trivial, converts sympathy into mockery.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'How wonderful, it is raining today of all days.'
questions.explanation: Que maravilha before a bad fact, plus logo before the time expression, marks the sentence as ironic.

activities.title: O dia perfeito
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. Everything has gone wrong today and you describe it to a friend entirely in ironic terms.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Então, como correu a viagem de trabalho?
lines.en: So, how did the work trip go?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Uma maravilha. O comboio atrasou-se uma hora, logo no dia da apresentação.
lines.en: Wonderful. The train was an hour late, on the day of the presentation of all days.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não me digas. E o hotel, era bom?
lines.en: You do not say. And the hotel, was it good?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ótimo, como se vê pela minha cara. O aquecimento não funcionava.
lines.en: Great, as you can tell from my face. The heating did not work.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Coitadinho, tiveste de dormir com um cobertor a mais.
lines.en: Poor little thing, you had to sleep with an extra blanket.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ri-te, ri-te. E de manhã ainda me disseram que o pequeno-almoço tinha acabado.
lines.en: Laugh away. And in the morning they told me breakfast was over.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Só faltava mais essa! Pelo menos a apresentação correu bem?
lines.en: That is all you needed! At least did the presentation go well?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Correu lindamente. Só me esqueci dos slides no computador de casa.
lines.en: It went beautifully. I just left the slides on the computer at home.

days.title: As expressões idiomáticas e os provérbios
days.objective: You can use a set of common European Portuguese idioms and proverbs correctly, and understand them when they appear without explanation.
days.paretoFocus: Idioms are the part of the language that cannot be worked out from the words, so a small memorised set unlocks a disproportionate amount of everyday speech.
drills: Choose three idioms and write a short situation for each where it would fit naturally.
Match each proverb you learn with an equivalent saying in your own language.
Retell something that went wrong this week using at least one idiom.

items: What does it mean to be left watching ships?
Which idiom describes making a blunder in public?
Which proverb advises patience over speed?

activities.title: Ficar a ver navios
activities.intro: Idioms are fixed: you cannot change the words or the verb tense without breaking them. The five below are heard constantly in Portugal and none of them can be guessed from the individual words. Learn each one attached to a concrete situation.

items.hr: Prometeram-lhe o lugar, mas ficou a ver navios.
items.en: They promised him the job, but he was left with nothing.
items.note: ficar a ver navios = to be left empty handed after a promise

items.hr: Para ele, um exame destes é canja.
items.en: For him, an exam like this is a piece of cake.
items.note: ser canja = to be very easy

items.hr: Devagar se vai ao longe.
items.en: Slow and steady wins the race.
items.note: proverb advising steady progress over haste

items.hr: O diretor meteu água na entrevista.
items.en: The director messed up in the interview.
items.note: meter água = to make a blunder, to say the wrong thing

items.hr: Quem não tem cão caça com gato.
items.en: You make do with what you have.
items.note: proverb about improvising with the means available

activities.title: Usar as expressões no contexto certo
activities.intro: Practise choosing the idiom that matches the situation.

questions.prompt: Someone was promised a promotion, waited a year and got nothing. Which idiom describes this?
questions.explanation: The expression refers to waiting for something promised that never arrives, so it fits a broken promise exactly.

questions.prompt: Which proverb fits someone who fixed a shelf with tape because they had no screws?
options: Quem não tem cão caça com gato.
Mais vale tarde do que nunca.
questions.answer: Quem não tem cão caça com gato.
questions.explanation: This proverb is specifically about improvising with whatever means are at hand rather than about timing or forgiveness.

questions.prompt: Complete the idiom meaning 'that is very easy for him': 'Para ele, isso é ___.'
questions.explanation: The word literally names a light chicken soup, and the idiom uses it to mean something requiring no effort.

questions.prompt: Complete the idiom for making a public blunder, using the pretérito perfeito of 'meter': 'O porta-voz ___ água na conferência de imprensa.'
questions.explanation: The image is of a boat taking on water; the verb stays in the same form as any ordinary past tense.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese proverb meaning: 'Slow and steady wins the race.'
questions.explanation: The proverb uses an impersonal se construction and its word order is fixed.

activities.title: As obras lá de casa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You tell a friend about renovation work that went badly, and you both use idioms and proverbs throughout.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Então, as obras já acabaram?
lines.en: So, is the building work finished?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nem por sombras. O empreiteiro prometeu acabar em maio e ficámos a ver navios.
lines.en: Not at all. The contractor promised to finish in May and we were left with nothing.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Já contavas com isso, não? Estas obras nunca são canja.
lines.en: You expected that, did you not? This kind of work is never a piece of cake.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pois não. E ele ainda meteu água quando explicou o orçamento aos vizinhos.
lines.en: It is not. And he even put his foot in it when he explained the budget to the neighbours.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Que disse ele?
lines.en: What did he say?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Disse que o material era outro, mas quem não tem cão caça com gato.
lines.en: He said the material was a different one, but you make do with what you have.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não te chateies. Devagar se vai ao longe.
lines.en: Do not get upset. Slow and steady wins the race.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: É o que eu digo a mim próprio todos os dias, enquanto olho para o teto por acabar.
lines.en: That is what I tell myself every day, while I look at the unfinished ceiling.

days.title: A comparação e a metáfora na escrita
days.objective: You can build similes with como and tal como, comparatives with do que, and simple metaphors by apposition, in order to make written descriptions vivid.
days.paretoFocus: One well placed image makes a paragraph memorable, and comparative structures with do que are also a frequent accuracy trap for learners.
drills: Describe your street, a queue and a meeting, each with one simile.
Write three comparatives about prices, distances and difficulty, all using do que.
Turn one of your similes into a metaphor by removing como.

items: Which word follows mais and menos in a comparison of two things?
What is the difference between a simile and a metaphor in practice?
What does tal como do at the start of an argumentative sentence?

activities.title: Como um rio de metal
activities.intro: A simile states the comparison openly with como or tal como. A metaphor drops the marker and simply names one thing as another, often through apposition after a comma. In comparatives, European Portuguese uses do que rather than que on its own before a second element.

items.hr: O trânsito arrastava-se como um rio de metal.
items.en: The traffic dragged along like a river of metal.
items.note: simile: the comparison is marked by como

items.hr: A cidade acordou coberta de nevoeiro, um lençol branco sobre os telhados.
items.en: The city woke up covered in fog, a white sheet over the rooftops.
items.note: metaphor by apposition: no marker, just a comma

items.hr: Tal como acontece noutros países, os preços subiram no verão.
items.en: Just as happens in other countries, prices went up in the summer.
items.note: tal como introduces a parallel in argumentative writing

items.hr: Isto é mais um remendo do que uma solução.
items.en: This is more of a patch than a solution.
items.note: mais ... do que: do que is required before the second term

items.hr: Este debate tornou-se um beco sem saída.
items.en: This debate has become a dead end.
items.note: beco sem saída is a common metaphor for a deadlock

activities.title: Imagens e comparações
activities.intro: Practise comparative structures and the difference between stated and implied images.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct comparison: 'Esta casa é mais cara ___ a outra.'
questions.explanation: Before a second noun phrase, European Portuguese uses do que after mais or menos; como would express equality, not superiority.

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a metaphor rather than a simile?
options: O ministro é a última barreira antes do caos.
O ministro fala como um professor cansado.
O ministro parece um professor cansado.
O ministro é tão calmo como o antecessor.
questions.answer: O ministro é a última barreira antes do caos.
questions.explanation: It identifies one thing directly with another without como or parecer, which is what makes it a metaphor.

questions.prompt: Complete the comparison with the two words required before the second term: 'A proposta é mais realista ___ ___ a anterior.'
questions.explanation: Mais plus an adjective is followed by do que when a second element is named.

questions.prompt: Complete the metaphor for a situation with no way out: 'As negociações chegaram a um ___ sem saída.'
questions.explanation: The word names a narrow street closed at one end, and the whole phrase works as a fixed metaphor for a deadlock.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'The traffic dragged along like a river of metal.'
questions.explanation: The imperfect arrastava-se describes an ongoing scene and como marks the simile.

activities.title: A crónica sobre a cidade
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are helping a friend improve a descriptive text, suggesting similes, metaphors and correct comparatives.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Escrevi uma crónica sobre a cidade de manhã, mas está seca. Ajudas-me?
lines.en: I wrote a piece about the city in the morning, but it is dry. Will you help me?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ajudo. Em vez de dizeres que havia muito trânsito, escreve que o trânsito se arrastava como um rio de metal.
lines.en: I will. Instead of saying there was a lot of traffic, write that the traffic dragged along like a river of metal.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Gosto. E o nevoeiro? Escrevi apenas que estava nevoeiro.
lines.en: I like that. And the fog? I only wrote that it was foggy.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tira o como e faz disso uma metáfora: um lençol branco sobre os telhados.
lines.en: Drop the like and turn it into a metaphor: a white sheet over the rooftops.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Fica mais forte, sem dúvida. Aqui comparei o bairro novo com o antigo.
lines.en: It is stronger, no doubt. Here I compared the new neighbourhood with the old one.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nessa frase escreveste mais barato que o outro. Em Portugal escreve-se mais barato do que o outro.
lines.en: In that sentence you wrote cheaper than the other one incorrectly. In Portugal you write it with do que.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Corrigido. E o fim, achas que resulta?
lines.en: Corrected. And the ending, do you think it works?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Resulta, mas é mais uma conclusão do que uma imagem. Tal como fizeste no início, fecha com uma metáfora.
lines.en: It works, but it is more a conclusion than an image. Just as you did at the beginning, close with a metaphor.

days.title: As construções impessoais
days.objective: You can build impersonal sentences with se, including with ser and with reflexive verbs, and know when the verb agrees with a following plural noun.
days.paretoFocus: Signs, rules, news and generalisations in Portugal are almost all written impersonally, so this one structure covers a huge slice of public language.
drills: Write five rules for a shared flat, all impersonal, none using tu or você.
Turn three personal opinions into impersonal generalisations about life in a city.
Copy three public notices you can recall and check whether the verb is singular or plural.

items: When does the verb in a se construction take a plural ending?
How do you make an impersonal sentence with the verb ser?
Where does se go when the sentence is negative?

activities.title: Fala-se muito disso
activities.intro: The particle se builds sentences with no named subject. With a transitive verb plus a plural noun, the verb agrees with that noun, which is why you see falam-se línguas. With intransitive verbs and with ser, the verb stays in the third person singular and the sentence means people in general.

items.hr: Fala-se muito deste assunto nos jornais.
items.en: This subject is talked about a lot in the newspapers.
items.note: the verb stays singular because deste assunto is a prepositional phrase

items.hr: Vendem-se apartamentos nesta rua.
items.en: Flats are for sale on this street.
items.note: with a plural noun as subject, the verb agrees: vendem-se

items.hr: Não se pode fumar aqui dentro.
items.en: Smoking is not allowed in here.
items.note: in a negative clause se comes before the verb

items.hr: É-se sempre estrangeiro nalgum sítio.
items.en: One is always a foreigner somewhere.
items.note: é-se: the impersonal built on ser, typical of reflective writing

items.hr: Chegou-se à conclusão de que era tarde de mais.
items.en: The conclusion was reached that it was too late.
items.note: the impersonal works in past tenses too: chegou-se

activities.title: Falar sem sujeito
activities.intro: Practise agreement and clitic placement in impersonal sentences.

questions.prompt: Choose the standard form for a sign advertising several flats: '___ apartamentos remodelados.'
questions.explanation: The plural noun apartamentos is the grammatical subject, so the verb takes the third person plural ending.

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the clitic correctly after a negation?
questions.explanation: A negative word attracts the clitic before the verb, so proclisis is obligatory here.

questions.prompt: Complete with the impersonal present of 'viver', written as one hyphenated form: '___ bem nesta zona da cidade.'
questions.explanation: With an intransitive verb the impersonal stays in the third person singular and the clitic attaches after it.

questions.prompt: Complete with the impersonal form of 'ser' plus the clitic, meaning 'one is': '___ sempre estrangeiro em algum lugar.'
questions.explanation: Ser also accepts the impersonal particle, giving a generalisation about people in general.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'People talk a lot about this subject.'
questions.explanation: There is no expressed subject, and the contraction deste comes from de plus este.

activities.title: As regras do prédio
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A new neighbour asks about the building rules and you explain them impersonally.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa tarde. Acabei de me mudar para o segundo andar. Há regras que eu deva saber?
lines.en: Good afternoon. I have just moved into the second floor. Are there rules I should know?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Há algumas. Não se faz barulho depois das onze da noite.
lines.en: There are a few. No noise is made after eleven at night.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Isso é fácil. E o lixo, como funciona?
lines.en: That is easy. And the rubbish, how does it work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Separa-se tudo em três contentores e não se deixam sacos na escada.
lines.en: Everything is separated into three bins and no bags are left on the stairs.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Percebido. E posso estender roupa na varanda da frente?
lines.en: Understood. And can I hang washing on the front balcony?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não se costuma fazer isso aqui, mas ninguém o proíbe formalmente.
lines.en: That is not usually done here, but nobody formally forbids it.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E as reuniões de condóminos, são frequentes?
lines.en: And the residents' meetings, are they frequent?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Duas por ano. Chegou-se à conclusão de que mais do que isso não resolvia nada.
lines.en: Two a year. The conclusion was reached that more than that solved nothing.

days.title: A frase complexa: a subordinação múltipla
days.objective: You can build and understand long sentences that stack several subordinate clauses, keeping the mood of each verb correct.
days.paretoFocus: Written argument in Portuguese runs on long multi-clause sentences, and the mood of each embedded verb is decided by its own conjunction, not by the main verb.
drills: Join four short sentences you already know into a single sentence with at least three clauses.
Write one sentence containing a concessive clause, a temporal clause and a purpose clause.
Take a long sentence from a Portuguese article and mark where each clause begins and ends.

items: Which mood does para que always require?
Which mood follows quem in a general statement about the future?
What happens to the mood inside a clause introduced by ainda que?

activities.title: Frases que se encaixam
activities.intro: In a complex sentence each conjunction governs its own clause and chooses its own mood. Concessive links like embora and ainda que take the conjuntivo, purpose links like para que take the conjuntivo, and a clause opened by quem referring to an unknown future person takes the future conjuntivo. The main clause mood is decided separately.

items.hr: Embora soubesse que ia chover, saiu sem guarda-chuva porque estava atrasado.
items.en: Although he knew it was going to rain, he went out without an umbrella because he was late.
items.note: concessive with imperfeito do conjuntivo plus a causal clause

items.hr: Quem disser que isto é simples ou não leu o relatório ou não o percebeu.
items.en: Whoever says this is simple either has not read the report or has not understood it.
items.note: quem plus futuro do conjuntivo, then a double alternative

items.hr: O que me preocupa é que, quando os prazos apertarem, ninguém saiba o que fazer.
items.en: What worries me is that, when the deadlines get tight, nobody will know what to do.
items.note: three clauses stacked, two of them in the conjuntivo

items.hr: Ainda que aceitem a proposta que lhes enviámos, será preciso rever o orçamento.
items.en: Even if they accept the proposal we sent them, the budget will have to be revised.
items.note: concessive plus a relative clause inside it

items.hr: Depois de terem discutido durante horas, adiaram a votação para que todos pudessem participar.
items.en: After arguing for hours, they postponed the vote so that everyone could take part.
items.note: infinitivo pessoal composto plus a purpose clause in the conjuntivo

activities.title: Encaixar orações
activities.intro: Practise choosing the right mood inside each embedded clause.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: 'Ainda que ___ a proposta, teremos de rever o orçamento.'
questions.explanation: Ainda que is concessive and always governs the conjuntivo, here the present conjuntivo because the main clause looks forward.

questions.prompt: In 'Adiaram tudo para que os colegas pudessem votar', why is 'pudessem' used?
options: para que requires the conjuntivo and the main verb is in the past
para que requires the indicative in past contexts
poder is irregular and has no indicative past
the clause expresses a certainty about the past
questions.answer: para que requires the conjuntivo and the main verb is in the past
questions.explanation: Purpose clauses take the conjuntivo, and the past main verb pushes it into the imperfeito do conjuntivo.

questions.prompt: Complete with the imperfeito do conjuntivo of 'saber': 'Embora ___ que ia chover, saiu sem guarda-chuva.'
questions.explanation: Embora governs the conjuntivo, and the past narrative context requires the imperfect form.

questions.prompt: Complete with the futuro do conjuntivo of 'dizer': 'Quem ___ que isto é fácil não leu o relatório.'
questions.explanation: Quem referring to an unspecified person in an open future situation takes the future conjuntivo.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'They postponed the vote so that everyone could take part.'
questions.explanation: The purpose clause follows the main clause and its verb is in the imperfeito do conjuntivo to match the past main verb.

activities.title: A reunião que nunca mais acaba
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague review a difficult decision, speaking in long sentences with several embedded clauses.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Explica-me outra vez porque é que a decisão foi adiada.
lines.en: Explain to me again why the decision was postponed.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Depois de terem discutido durante duas horas, adiaram tudo para que a direção pudesse ler o parecer.
lines.en: After arguing for two hours, they postponed everything so that the board could read the opinion.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E se a direção não ler nada até sexta-feira?
lines.en: And if the board does not read anything by Friday?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Quem não ler o parecer até sexta terá de aceitar o que os outros decidirem na reunião seguinte.
lines.en: Whoever does not read the opinion by Friday will have to accept whatever the others decide at the next meeting.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ainda que aceitem, alguém vai reclamar do orçamento.
lines.en: Even if they accept, somebody is going to complain about the budget.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Embora soubesse disso desde o início, o coordenador preferiu não avisar ninguém porque temia o conflito.
lines.en: Although he knew that from the start, the coordinator preferred not to warn anyone because he feared the conflict.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: O que me preocupa é que, quando os prazos apertarem, ninguém assuma a responsabilidade.
lines.en: What worries me is that, when the deadlines get tight, nobody will take responsibility.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Concordo, e por isso proponho que escrevamos tudo por email antes que alguém mude de ideias.
lines.en: I agree, and that is why I propose we put everything in writing by email before anyone changes their mind.

days.title: A anáfora: evitar repetições
days.objective: You can refer back to something already mentioned using clitics, demonstratives, the summarising word tal and the substitute o outro, instead of repeating nouns.
days.paretoFocus: Repeating the same noun in consecutive sentences is the clearest sign of a learner text, and Portuguese has cheap, fixed devices to avoid it.
drills: Take a paragraph you wrote and delete every repeated noun, replacing it with a pronoun or a demonstrative.
Write two sentences where the second one starts with tal plus a noun that sums up the first.
Practise the contracted forms of fazer plus the object clitic until they come automatically.

items: What form does fazer take before the clitic o?
Which demonstrative refers to the second of two people just mentioned?
How can tal replace a whole previous sentence?

activities.title: Não repetir o mesmo nome
activities.intro: Portuguese avoids repetition with a small toolkit: object clitics, the pair este and aquele for the latter and the former, the substitute o outro, and tal plus a noun to summarise a whole previous statement. Note that verbs ending in a vowel sound change shape before o and a, giving forms such as fê-lo.

items.hr: Comprei dois livros; um é sobre história, o outro sobre economia.
items.en: I bought two books; one is about history, the other about economics.
items.note: o outro replaces the repeated noun livro

items.hr: A proposta foi rejeitada. Tal decisão surpreendeu toda a gente.
items.en: The proposal was rejected. That decision surprised everyone.
items.note: tal plus a noun summarises the whole previous sentence

items.hr: Falei com o João e com a Rita; esta trabalha num hospital.
items.en: I spoke to João and to Rita; the latter works in a hospital.
items.note: esta points to the nearest, that is the last one mentioned

items.hr: Não li o relatório, mas o meu colega leu-o ontem.
items.en: I did not read the report, but my colleague read it yesterday.
items.note: the clitic o stands for o relatório

items.hr: Prometeu mudar de atitude e fê-lo sem hesitar.
items.en: He promised to change his attitude and did so without hesitating.
items.note: fez plus o gives fê-lo, meaning did so

activities.title: Substituir em vez de repetir
activities.intro: Practise the devices that carry reference across sentences.

questions.prompt: Why does 'fazer' appear as 'fê-lo' in 'Prometeu ajudar e fê-lo logo'?
options: The verb ends in a consonant sound, so the clitic becomes lo and the vowel changes.
It is an irregular imperative form of fazer.
The hyphen marks a future tense with an inserted pronoun.
It is the impersonal form of fazer with se.
questions.answer: The verb ends in a consonant sound, so the clitic becomes lo and the vowel changes.
questions.explanation: Forms ending in r, s or z drop that consonant, the clitic o becomes lo, and the remaining vowel takes an accent, giving fê-lo.

questions.prompt: Which version avoids repetition most effectively?
options: O plano foi aprovado. Tal decisão agradou aos moradores.
O plano foi aprovado. O plano agradou aos moradores.
O plano foi aprovado. A aprovação do plano agradou aos moradores.
O plano foi aprovado. O plano aprovado agradou aos moradores.
questions.answer: O plano foi aprovado. Tal decisão agradou aos moradores.
questions.explanation: Tal plus a summarising noun refers back to the whole first sentence without repeating any of its words.

questions.prompt: Complete with the form of 'fazer' joined to the object clitic, meaning 'and did so': 'Prometeu mudar e ___ no dia seguinte.'
questions.explanation: Fez loses the final z, the clitic takes the form lo, and the e receives a circumflex.

questions.prompt: Complete with the demonstrative that points to the second person mentioned: 'Falei com o Pedro e com a Sofia; ___ trabalha num hospital.'
questions.explanation: Este and esta refer to the nearest element, that is the last one named, while aquele and aquela refer to the first.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'Such a decision surprised everyone.'
questions.explanation: Tal comes before the noun with no article and toda a gente is the usual way of saying everyone in Portugal.

activities.title: A revisão do texto
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are correcting a friend's repetitive text and suggesting ways to refer back without repeating.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Leste o meu texto? Disseram-me que é repetitivo.
lines.en: Did you read my text? They told me it is repetitive.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Li-o ontem à noite. O problema é que repetes a palavra proposta seis vezes.
lines.en: I read it last night. The problem is that you repeat the word proposal six times.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E como é que evito isso sem tornar o texto confuso?
lines.en: And how do I avoid that without making the text confusing?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Na segunda frase escreve tal proposta ou simplesmente tal decisão, e o leitor percebe.
lines.en: In the second sentence write that proposal or simply that decision, and the reader understands.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Aqui mencionei dois autores e depois repeti os nomes.
lines.en: Here I mentioned two authors and then repeated the names.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Usa aquele para o primeiro e este para o segundo. Fica mais leve.
lines.en: Use aquele for the first and este for the second. It reads more lightly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E na conclusão escrevi que ele prometeu rever o texto e reviu o texto.
lines.en: And in the conclusion I wrote that he promised to revise the text and revised the text.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Aí escreve simplesmente que prometeu rever e fê-lo. Não precisas de mais nada.
lines.en: There just write that he promised to revise and did so. You need nothing else.

days.title: A pontuação e o ritmo da frase
days.objective: You can punctuate Portuguese sentences so that commas, colons and semicolons carry the meaning you intend, especially in relative clauses.
days.paretoFocus: In Portuguese a pair of commas can change who a sentence is talking about, so punctuation is grammar rather than decoration.
drills: Write the same relative clause twice, once with commas and once without, and explain the difference in meaning.
Punctuate a list of four items using a colon and commas.
Rewrite a long comma-only sentence using one semicolon to improve the rhythm.

items: What changes when you put commas around a relative clause?
Which mark introduces a list or an explanation?
What is the Portuguese name of the mark that joins two closely linked clauses?

activities.title: As vírgulas mudam o sentido
activities.intro: Commas around a relative clause make it non-defining, meaning it applies to the whole group. Without commas the clause restricts the group to a subset. Colons introduce lists and explanations, and the semicolon joins two independent clauses that belong together.

items.hr: Os alunos, que estavam cansados, saíram mais cedo.
items.en: The students, who were tired, left earlier.
items.note: with commas: all the students were tired and all left

items.hr: Os alunos que estavam cansados saíram mais cedo.
items.en: The students who were tired left earlier.
items.note: without commas: only the tired ones left

items.hr: Não sei, disse ela, se vale a pena insistir.
items.en: I do not know, she said, whether it is worth insisting.
items.note: a pair of commas frames the reporting clause inside the speech

items.hr: Havia três coisas por resolver: o horário, o orçamento e a sala.
items.en: There were three things left to sort out: the timetable, the budget and the room.
items.note: the colon announces the list that explains the preceding noun

items.hr: Ele hesitou; depois, sem dizer nada, assinou.
items.en: He hesitated; then, without saying anything, he signed.
items.note: the semicolon links two closely connected independent clauses

activities.title: Pontuar com intenção
activities.intro: Practise the meaning differences that punctuation creates.

questions.prompt: What does 'Os passageiros que tinham bagagem esperaram mais tempo' tell us?
options: Only some passengers had luggage, and only those waited longer.
All the passengers had luggage and all waited longer.
Nobody had luggage but everyone waited.
The passengers waited before receiving their luggage.
questions.answer: Only some passengers had luggage, and only those waited longer.
questions.explanation: Without commas the relative clause restricts the noun, so it identifies a subset of the passengers.

questions.prompt: Which punctuation mark announces a list that explains what came before it?
questions.explanation: The colon introduces an enumeration or an explanation of the element immediately preceding it.

questions.prompt: In Portuguese, the mark written as ':' is called ___ ___ (two words).
questions.explanation: It is named after its two dots and it introduces lists, explanations and direct speech.

questions.prompt: Complete the Portuguese name of the mark ';' which joins two closely linked independent clauses: 'ponto e ___'.
questions.explanation: The name combines the words for full stop and comma, matching its intermediate strength.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'He hesitated; then, without saying anything, he signed.'
questions.explanation: The semicolon separates the two actions and the pair of commas isolates the adverbial phrase inside the second clause.

activities.title: As vírgulas do comunicado
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You and a colleague argue about the punctuation of an official notice, because the commas change who it applies to.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Escrevi assim: os funcionários, que trabalham ao sábado, recebem um suplemento.
lines.en: I wrote it like this: the employees, who work on Saturdays, receive a supplement.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Com essas vírgulas estás a dizer que todos trabalham ao sábado. É isso que queres?
lines.en: With those commas you are saying that all of them work on Saturdays. Is that what you mean?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Não, só alguns é que trabalham. Então tiro as vírgulas?
lines.en: No, only some of them work. So I remove the commas?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tiras. Sem vírgulas, a frase limita o suplemento a quem trabalha ao sábado.
lines.en: You do. Without commas, the sentence limits the supplement to those who work on Saturdays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Percebido. E depois tenho uma lista de condições. Uso vírgula antes?
lines.en: Understood. And then I have a list of conditions. Do I use a comma before it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Aí usa dois pontos: horário, antiguidade e função. Fica muito mais claro.
lines.en: There use a colon: hours, seniority and role. It is much clearer.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E este parágrafo final é longo de mais, com cinco vírgulas seguidas.
lines.en: And this final paragraph is too long, with five commas in a row.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Parte-o com um ponto e vírgula; a segunda parte respira melhor assim.
lines.en: Split it with a semicolon; the second half breathes better that way.

days.title: Os falsos amigos e as armadilhas do português europeu
days.objective: You can avoid the most common false friends in Portuguese and use the European vocabulary for everyday objects and transport.
days.paretoFocus: These few words appear daily on doors, timetables and in small talk, and getting them wrong produces immediate confusion rather than a mild accent.
drills: Write five sentences, each containing one false friend used correctly.
List the European words for train, bus, mobile phone, bathroom and breakfast and use each in a sentence.
Note any word you have been using that belongs to another variety of Portuguese and replace it.

items: Does puxar mean to push or to pull?
What does pretender actually mean?
Which verb describes catching a cold in Portugal?

activities.title: Cuidado com estas palavras
activities.intro: Some Portuguese words look like English ones but mean something different, and some everyday items have a specific European name. Both types cause real misunderstandings. Learn them as pairs: the trap and the correct meaning.

items.hr: Pretendo mudar de emprego este ano.
items.en: I intend to change jobs this year.
items.note: pretender means to intend, never to pretend

items.hr: O comboio das sete está outra vez atrasado.
items.en: The seven o'clock train is late again.
items.note: comboio is the word for train used in Portugal

items.hr: Puxe a porta, por favor.
items.en: Pull the door, please.
items.note: puxar means to pull; empurrar means to push

items.hr: Ele é muito esperto, resolveu aquilo sozinho.
items.en: He is very clever, he solved that on his own.
items.note: esperto means clever or sharp, not expert

items.hr: Constipei-me com o frio de ontem.
items.en: I caught a cold from yesterday's cold weather.
items.note: constipar-se means to catch a cold, nothing else

activities.title: Não cair nas armadilhas
activities.intro: Practise the words that most often trip up learners in Portugal.

questions.prompt: A sign on a shop door says PUXE. What should you do?
questions.explanation: Puxar means to pull; the verb for pushing is empurrar, which often appears on the other side of the same door.

questions.prompt: What does someone mean by 'Pretendo candidatar-me a esse lugar'?
options: They intend to apply for the position.
They are pretending to apply for the position.
They have already been given the position.
questions.answer: They intend to apply for the position.
questions.explanation: Pretender expresses intention; pretending would be fingir.

questions.prompt: Complete with the European Portuguese word for a mobile phone: 'Esqueci-me do ___ em casa.'
questions.explanation: Telemóvel is the standard term in Portugal for a mobile phone.

questions.prompt: Complete with the verb used in Portugal for catching a cold, in the pretérito perfeito, first person singular with the clitic attached: '___ por causa da chuva.'
questions.explanation: The verb is reflexive and the clitic attaches after it in an affirmative main clause.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'The train to Porto is late again.'
questions.explanation: Comboio is the European word for train and the city name Porto takes the definite article.

activities.title: O primeiro dia em Lisboa
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A visitor keeps using the wrong words and you correct them gently while giving practical advice.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Desculpa, onde é que apanho o trem para Sintra?
lines.en: Sorry, where do I catch the train to Sintra?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Aqui dizemos comboio. Apanhas o comboio no Rossio, de dez em dez minutos.
lines.en: Here we say comboio. You catch the train at Rossio, every ten minutes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Obrigado. E esta porta, tenho de empurrar?
lines.en: Thank you. And this door, do I have to push it?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não, diz puxe. Se empurrares, não abre.
lines.en: No, it says pull. If you push, it will not open.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa. Pretendo ficar duas semanas, se o tempo ajudar.
lines.en: Good. I intend to stay two weeks, if the weather is kind.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Traz casaco, então. Com este vento, constipas-te num instante.
lines.en: Bring a coat, then. With this wind, you will catch a cold in no time.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Já me avisaram. Ah, e perdi o carregador do meu celular.
lines.en: I have been warned. Oh, and I lost the charger for my mobile.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Telemóvel, aqui. Há uma loja ali em frente que vende carregadores.
lines.en: Telemóvel, here. There is a shop right opposite that sells chargers.

days.title: As contrações avançadas e a crase
days.objective: You can write the less obvious contractions of Portuguese, including àquele, nalgum, doutro and pelo, and use the accent that marks the merger of a with a following a.
days.paretoFocus: These fused forms are compulsory in writing, and a missing grave accent changes the meaning of a sentence rather than just looking untidy.
drills: Write six sentences that each require a different contraction of a preposition with a determiner.
Take three sentences with a plus a feminine noun and add the grave accent where needed.
Practise reading aloud sentences with àquele and àquilo until the merged vowel feels natural.

items: When does the preposition a take a grave accent?
What happens when em meets algum?
How do you contract de with outra?

activities.title: Àquele, nalgum, doutro
activities.intro: Portuguese fuses prepositions with articles, demonstratives and indefinites. When the preposition a meets a feminine article or a word beginning with a, the two vowels merge into one written with a grave accent. European Portuguese also fuses em and de with algum and outro, which is standard in writing.

items.hr: Vou à reunião às três da tarde.
items.en: I am going to the meeting at three in the afternoon.
items.note: a + a gives à; a + as gives às

items.hr: Referiu-se àquele relatório que discutimos na semana passada.
items.en: He referred to that report we discussed last week.
items.note: a + aquele gives àquele

items.hr: As chaves devem estar nalguma gaveta da cozinha.
items.en: The keys must be in some kitchen drawer.
items.note: em + alguma gives nalguma, normal in Portugal

items.hr: Ele saiu doutra porta, para não ser visto.
items.en: He left by another door, so as not to be seen.
items.note: de + outra gives doutra

items.hr: Falámos disso pelo telefone, não pessoalmente.
items.en: We talked about it on the phone, not in person.
items.note: de + isso gives disso; por + o gives pelo

activities.title: Juntar as preposições
activities.intro: Practise producing the fused forms accurately, including the accent.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct form: 'O relator referiu-se ___ questões levantadas pelos moradores.'
questions.explanation: The verb requires the preposition a, and a plus the feminine plural article as merges into às with a grave accent.

questions.prompt: Which form results from joining 'em' with 'algum'?
questions.explanation: Em fuses with the indefinite exactly as it does with articles, giving nalgum in the masculine singular.

questions.prompt: Complete by joining the preposition 'a' with the demonstrative 'aquele': 'O advogado referiu-se ___ documento durante a audiência.'
questions.explanation: The two a sounds merge into one vowel, written with a grave accent to mark the fusion.

questions.prompt: Complete by joining the preposition 'de' with 'outra': 'Saiu ___ porta, sem que ninguém o visse.'
questions.explanation: De fuses with outra in written European Portuguese, giving a single word.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'We are going to the meeting at three o'clock.'
questions.explanation: Both the destination and the clock time require the fused form with a grave accent.

activities.title: A marcação da consulta
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are arranging an appointment on the phone and both speakers use contracted forms constantly.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Boa tarde. Queria marcar uma consulta para a próxima semana.
lines.en: Good afternoon. I would like to book an appointment for next week.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Com certeza. Prefere à terça-feira de manhã ou às quintas ao fim do dia?
lines.en: Certainly. Do you prefer Tuesday morning or Thursdays at the end of the day?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Às quintas é melhor. Refiro-me àquele horário depois das seis.
lines.en: Thursdays are better. I mean that slot after six.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Tenho às seis e meia. Traga consigo os exames, se estiverem nalguma pasta lá de casa.
lines.en: I have half past six. Bring the test results with you, if they are in some folder at home.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Acho que ficaram no consultório doutro médico, o do ano passado.
lines.en: I think they were left at another doctor's practice, last year's one.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não faz mal. Pedimos cópias pelo correio ou tratamos disso na consulta.
lines.en: That is not a problem. We will request copies by post or we will deal with that at the appointment.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Obrigado. E se eu precisar de desmarcar?
lines.en: Thank you. And if I need to cancel?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Telefone até às cinco do dia anterior e passamos para outra semana.
lines.en: Call by five o'clock the day before and we will move it to another week.

days.title: O gerúndio e as perífrases verbais em Portugal
days.objective: You can use estar a plus infinitive and the other European verbal periphrases correctly, and recognise where the gerund still survives in writing.
days.paretoFocus: Using the gerund for ongoing actions is the single most recognisable mark of the wrong variety of Portuguese, and the periphrases replace it in every spoken context.
drills: Describe five things happening around you right now, all with estar a plus infinitive.
Write three sentences with andar a, continuar a and acabar de.
Find one written sentence where a gerund would still sound natural and explain why.

items: How do you say what you are doing right now in Portugal?
What does andar a plus infinitive add compared with estar a?
Which periphrasis expresses that something has just happened?

activities.title: Estou a fazer, não estou fazendo
activities.intro: European Portuguese expresses ongoing action with estar a plus the infinitive. The gerund is not wrong in itself, but in Portugal it survives mainly in written descriptions of a simultaneous action. Several other periphrases carry aspect: andar a for a repeated habit, continuar a for persistence and acabar de for the immediate past.

items.hr: Não posso falar agora, estou a estudar para o exame.
items.en: I cannot talk now, I am studying for the exam.
items.note: estar a plus infinitive is the standard progressive in Portugal

items.hr: Saiu a correr, gritando o nome dela.
items.en: He ran out, shouting her name.
items.note: the gerund survives for a simultaneous action, mostly in writing

items.hr: Continuo a pensar no que me disseste ontem.
items.en: I keep thinking about what you told me yesterday.
items.note: continuar a plus infinitive means to keep on doing

items.hr: Ele anda a treinar todos os dias para a corrida.
items.en: He has been training every day for the race.
items.note: andar a plus infinitive marks a repeated habit over a period

items.hr: Acabei de chegar, dá-me cinco minutos.
items.en: I have just arrived, give me five minutes.
items.note: acabar de plus infinitive expresses the immediate past

activities.title: As perífrases do dia a dia
activities.intro: Practise choosing the periphrasis that matches the aspect you mean.

questions.prompt: How would someone in Portugal normally ask 'What are you doing?'
options: O que é que estás a fazer?
O que é que estás fazendo?
O que é que fazes a fazer?
O que é que andas fazer?
questions.answer: O que é que estás a fazer?
questions.explanation: The European progressive is estar plus a plus the infinitive; the gerund version belongs to another variety.

questions.prompt: What does 'Ele anda a procurar casa' tell us?
options: He has been looking for a place for some time now.
He is looking for a place at this precise moment.
He has just found a place.
He walks past houses on his way to work.
questions.answer: He has been looking for a place for some time now.
questions.explanation: Andar a plus infinitive expresses a habit spread over a period rather than an action happening right now.

questions.prompt: Complete with the pretérito perfeito of 'acabar', first person singular, to express the immediate past: '___ de chegar a casa.'
questions.explanation: This verb plus de plus an infinitive is the European way of saying you have just done something.

questions.prompt: Complete with the third person singular present of the verb meaning 'to walk', used here for a habit spread over a period: 'Ele ___ a treinar todos os dias.'
questions.explanation: This verb plus a plus an infinitive covers a stretch of time rather than the present instant.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'I am studying for the exam.'
questions.explanation: The preposition a is obligatory between estar and the infinitive in European Portuguese.

activities.title: O telefonema a meio da tarde
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A friend calls while you are busy, and the whole conversation runs on European verbal periphrases.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Estou? Estás ocupado? O que é que estás a fazer?
lines.en: Hello? Are you busy? What are you doing?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Estou a acabar um relatório. Acabei de receber os últimos números.
lines.en: I am finishing a report. I have just received the latest figures.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Então ligo mais tarde. Ainda andas a trabalhar naquele projeto de janeiro?
lines.en: I will call later then. Are you still working on that project from January?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ando, sim. Continuo a acreditar que vale a pena, apesar dos atrasos.
lines.en: I am, yes. I still believe it is worth it, despite the delays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Admiro-te a paciência. Eu tinha desistido logo no primeiro mês.
lines.en: I admire your patience. I would have given up in the first month.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Há dias em que estou quase a desistir, confesso. Depois passa.
lines.en: There are days when I am on the point of giving up, I admit. Then it passes.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Vamos jantar fora logo? A tua irmã anda a dizer que nunca te vê.
lines.en: Shall we go out for dinner later? Your sister keeps saying she never sees you.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Vamos. Às oito estou a sair do escritório, telefono-te quando estiver na rua.
lines.en: Let us do that. At eight I will be leaving the office, I will call you when I am outside.

days.title: A ordem das palavras e a ênfase
days.objective: You can highlight any element of a Portuguese sentence using cleft structures with ser and que, pseudo-clefts with o que, and topic fronting with a resumptive clitic.
days.paretoFocus: Portuguese marks emphasis by restructuring the sentence rather than by stressing a word, so without these patterns your speech sounds flat and your meaning gets lost.
drills: Take one plain sentence and rewrite it three times, emphasising the subject, the time and the object in turn.
Write three questions in the European style with the emphatic sequence after the question word.
Front the object of two sentences and add the clitic that resumes it.

items: How do you highlight the time of an event using ser?
What structure begins with o que and ends with é?
What must you add to the verb when you front the object?

activities.title: Foi ontem que eles chegaram
activities.intro: Portuguese emphasises by rearranging the sentence. The cleft ser plus que isolates the highlighted element, the pseudo-cleft o que ... é introduces what matters at the end, and fronting a phrase to the start marks it as the topic, usually with a clitic later in the clause to resume it.

items.hr: Foi ontem que eles chegaram.
items.en: It was yesterday that they arrived.
items.note: cleft with ser plus que highlights the time

items.hr: O que eu quero é descansar uns dias.
items.en: What I want is to rest for a few days.
items.note: pseudo-cleft: the key information lands at the end

items.hr: Quando é que a reunião começa?
items.en: When does the meeting start?
items.note: the sequence after the question word is standard in Portugal

items.hr: Ao João, ninguém lhe disse nada.
items.en: As for João, nobody told him anything.
items.note: fronted topic resumed by the clitic lhe

items.hr: Dinheiro, eles têm; tempo é que não.
items.en: Money, they have; time they do not.
items.note: two fronted topics in contrast, the second reinforced by é que

activities.title: Pôr em destaque
activities.intro: Practise moving the emphasis to different parts of the sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence puts the emphasis specifically on the person responsible?
options: Foi a Ana que resolveu o problema.
A Ana resolveu o problema ontem.
questions.answer: Foi a Ana que resolveu o problema.
questions.explanation: The cleft with ser plus que isolates the subject and presents it as the answer to the question of who did it.

questions.prompt: Which question sounds most natural in everyday European Portuguese?
questions.explanation: The emphatic sequence after the question word is the ordinary spoken pattern in Portugal and needs no inversion.

questions.prompt: Complete the cleft that highlights the day: 'Foi ontem ___ eles chegaram de Lisboa.'
questions.explanation: The cleft always pairs a form of ser with this invariable linker before the rest of the clause.

questions.prompt: Complete the pseudo-cleft with the correct form of 'ser': 'O que eu quero ___ descansar uns dias.'
questions.explanation: The pseudo-cleft joins the two halves with the third person singular present of ser.

questions.prompt: Build the European Portuguese for: 'As for João, nobody told him anything.'
questions.explanation: The indirect object is fronted with its preposition and resumed by the clitic lhe, which sits before the verb because ninguém attracts it.

activities.title: Quem é que decidiu isto?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are getting to the bottom of a decision at work, and both speakers use cleft and fronted structures to press each point.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Quem é que decidiu mudar a data da apresentação?
lines.en: Who decided to change the date of the presentation?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Foi a direção que decidiu, na sexta-feira à tarde.
lines.en: It was the board that decided, on Friday afternoon.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E a nós, ninguém nos avisou?
lines.en: And as for us, did nobody warn us?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Avisaram por email, mas foi só ontem que a mensagem chegou.
lines.en: They gave notice by email, but it was only yesterday that the message arrived.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Tempo para reagir é que não tivemos, portanto.
lines.en: Time to react is exactly what we did not have, then.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Pois não. O que eu quero agora é perceber quem fica responsável pelos slides.
lines.en: We did not. What I want now is to work out who is responsible for the slides.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ao Miguel, já lhe pedi ajuda. Ele trata da parte técnica.
lines.en: As for Miguel, I have already asked him for help. He handles the technical side.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ótimo. Então é a mim que cabe escrever a introdução até quarta.
lines.en: Great. So it falls to me to write the introduction by Wednesday.

days.title: A inteligência artificial e a sociedade
days.objective: You can concede a point about artificial intelligence and then push back, using concessive clauses with the subjunctive.
days.paretoFocus: Conceding before objecting is the single most useful move in any Portuguese debate, and it always needs the subjunctive.
drills: Write three sentences beginning 'Por mais ... que ...' about technology at work.
Take an opinion you disagree with and concede it first, then object, in one sentence.
Say aloud five verbs in the present subjunctive that you would use after 'ainda que'.

items: Which mood follows 'por muito que' and why?
How do you say 'however sophisticated the system may be'?
Give two ways to admit a risk without agreeing with the conclusion.

activities.title: Conceder antes de contestar
activities.intro: Concessive clauses admit something before you argue against it. In Portuguese they take the subjunctive: 'por mais ... que', 'por muito que', 'ainda que', 'mesmo que'. The pattern 'por mais + adjective + que + subjunctive' is the most flexible of them all.

items.hr: Por mais sofisticados que sejam os algoritmos, continuam a depender dos dados que lhes damos.
items.en: However sophisticated the algorithms may be, they still depend on the data we give them.
items.note: por mais + adjective + que + present subjunctive (ser -> sejam)

items.hr: Ainda que a automação destrua empregos, também cria profissões que hoje nem imaginamos.
items.en: Even though automation destroys jobs, it also creates professions we cannot even imagine today.
items.note: ainda que + subjunctive (destruir -> destrua)

items.hr: Por muito que se legisle, a tecnologia avança sempre mais depressa do que o direito.
items.en: However much we legislate, technology always moves faster than the law.
items.note: por muito que + subjunctive; próclise after 'que'

items.hr: Não nego que haja riscos, mas convém distinguir o receio legítimo do alarmismo.
items.en: I do not deny that there are risks, but it is worth distinguishing legitimate fear from alarmism.
items.note: negar in the negative takes the subjunctive: haja

items.hr: Mesmo que os modelos sejam transparentes, ninguém garante que as decisões sejam justas.
items.en: Even if the models are transparent, nobody guarantees that the decisions are fair.
items.note: mesmo que = even if, hypothetical concession

activities.title: Concessivas em debate
activities.intro: Practise conceding a point and then objecting.

questions.prompt: Complete: 'Por mais dados ___ se recolham, o enviesamento não desaparece sozinho.'
questions.explanation: The concessive frame is 'por mais + noun + que + subjunctive'. The linking word is always 'que'.

questions.prompt: Which sentence concedes a point correctly before objecting to it?
options: Embora a máquina decide depressa, não explica as suas decisões.
Embora a máquina decida depressa, não explica as suas decisões.
Embora a máquina decidiu depressa, não explica as suas decisões.
Embora a máquina decidirá depressa, não explica as suas decisões.
questions.answer: Embora a máquina decida depressa, não explica as suas decisões.
questions.explanation: 'Embora' always governs the subjunctive, so 'decidir' becomes 'decida'.

questions.prompt: Complete with the correct form of 'ser': 'Ainda que a decisão ___ automática, alguém tem de responder por ela.'
questions.explanation: 'Ainda que' requires the present subjunctive of 'ser', which is 'seja'.

questions.prompt: Complete the concession: 'Por ___ que se invista em regulação, as empresas encontram sempre uma brecha.'
questions.explanation: With a verb rather than an adjective, the frame is 'por muito que + subjunctive', meaning 'however much'.

questions.prompt: Build the Portuguese for: 'However transparent the system may be, citizens deserve an explanation.'
questions.explanation: The concessive clause comes first, with the subjunctive 'seja', and the main clause follows after the comma.

activities.title: Vamos ser todos substituídos?
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. A colleague fears automation and you concede each point before objecting, using concessive clauses with the subjunctive.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Daqui a dez anos, metade destes postos de trabalho já não existe.
lines.en: Ten years from now, half of these jobs will no longer exist.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Por muito que se repita esse número, ninguém consegue mostrar de onde ele vem.
lines.en: However much that figure gets repeated, nobody can show where it comes from.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Mas tu próprio já usas a máquina para escrever relatórios.
lines.en: But you yourself already use the machine to write reports.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Uso, sim. Ainda que me poupe tempo, continuo a ser eu a decidir o que fica.
lines.en: I do, yes. Even though it saves me time, I am still the one deciding what stays.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: E se um dia a decisão for toda dela?
lines.en: And what if one day the decision is entirely hers?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Mesmo que isso aconteça, alguém terá de responder pelos erros. A responsabilidade não se automatiza.
lines.en: Even if that happens, someone will have to answer for the mistakes. Responsibility cannot be automated.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Então não estás nada preocupado.
lines.en: So you are not worried at all.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Não nego que haja motivos de preocupação. Só acho que estamos a temer a coisa errada.
lines.en: I do not deny that there are reasons to worry. I just think we are fearing the wrong thing.

days.title: A bioética e os limites da ciência
